info rather than debug after repeating failed retries. If you'd prefer, I
can also elevate it back to exception after x failed retries. I didn't do

First, our hypothetical user who wants to wield the tool multiple times can do so as easily as building that input list with multiple calls and the inplace kwarg.  Second, the output logic would branch much further; off the top of my head it would be dangerous to allow input like this and also permit indices to be true for memory reasons.  Third, with tolerance available the result of successive fills is not commutative and this will result in user confusion.

If such functionality would be desired it would entail a simple wrapper for any specific use case, but making it general is not trivial - and the added logic would slow its use as a low level tool.  If we wanted to ship a multi_fill function in the future that would be fine, exporting all of these issues to a separate function and discussion.

Its a bit of a shame that we need this - but AFAICT the issue is that a boundmethod instance still has `__get__` on it?

```
class patch(object):
   @classmethod
   def kls(cls, tgtclass, one): pass

patch.kls(1,2)
patch.kls # <- bound method - an instancemethod instance
patch().kls # - equally.
```
We got those from `__get__`.

where t is an instance of a class:
```
>>> t.z = patch.kls
>>> t.z(1)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: kls() takes exactly 3 arguments (2 given)
>>> t.z(1,2)
>>> type(t).z = patch.kls
>>> t.z()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: kls() takes exactly 3 arguments (1 given)
```
The first case above we're calling a callable directly, and the class of t won't be accessible.
In the second case, our descriptor is being unwrapped by t, so the class of patch won't be accessible - and we're breaking the model of being a simple callable.

But, if we wrap them with -any- proxy at all, we prevent that, no?
```
>>> t.x = classmethod(patch.kls)
>>> t.x(1)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: 'classmethod' object is not callable
```
^- this is because we assigned to the instance, so no descriptor logic gets invoked.

```
>>> type(t).x = classmethod(patch.kls)
>>> t.x(1)
>>>
```
OTOH, that means we're now assigning to the type when a user wants to patch a single instance.

But, AFAICT your code depends on descriptor logic too operate (assigning a bound method to an instance attribute doesn't need to do anything special because `__get__` won't be called). So - can't we just wrap directly in classmethod etc?

One possible sequence of events is:
1. user creates a BUILD file containing a target named `one`
2. user runs pants list, sees `one`
3. user renames the target from `one` to `two`
4. user runs pants list, sees `two` (which they're expecting, because they renamed the target)
5. etc.

That's all this is, except the loop is running `list` for you when it sees that files have changed.

Why does the user see `"two"` at step 4? Because the engine invalidates Nodes in the graph when you change files.

The TabStop_ mock gets called here every time your code calls TabStop(); setting up that class mock replaces the TabStop object in the tabstops module with TabStop_. When TabStop_ gets called, it doesn't create a TabStop object, but it does remember every time it was called and with what. So when we test `TabStop_.assert_called_once_with(tab)`, we're testing to make sure our code called TabStop exactly once, and passed the w:tab element of interest as a parameter.

A mock also has a discoverable return value on .return_value. So we set TabStop_.return_value to the tab_stop_ mock (that's done in the component definition), then we can test that our code returned the return value of TabStop, as it should.

I think unfortunately we're going to have to add support for another type of plugin, which is an extractor plugin. I think in a lot of cases the filenames in people's submissions won't have their usernames in them -- rather, the username will be at a higher level of the directory structure. For example, for Berkeley, we download a giant zip file of all submissions which when extracted has the directory structure like this:

```
Problem Set 1/LAST, FIRST(123456)/Submission attachment(s)/name_of_submission.zip
```

Of course, I could write a separate script to copy these zip files into the `downloaded` directory and rename them. But even them, the files inside won't have the right files names. Even better would be if there was an extractor plugin that could handle both (1) associating the student names with the zipfiles even though there is a directory hierarchy and (2) rename the files within the archive to have student names.

I think you misunderstand my intent.

NONE means NONE, as in NO OUTPUT at all.

the _NONE case means - all log output is completely disabled.  The intent in *THIS* case here (cli/main.c, and ncp/main.c) is to provide a *DUMMY* stub function that performs no work.  

Technically we could remove this 'dummy-stub' if every place on the platform has proper conditionals around their call to the log function that disabled/removed that call.  If it is true that all locations in the application do not call this function then there is no "call-graph-arc" into this function. We can then rely on the common compiler/linker options of:   -ffunction-section (which not all compilers support) which will remove unused functions from the code.

In the worse case scenario (some code is stupid and still calls this) - it becomes a dummy function that i a few bytes in size, in the grand scheme of things - 2 to 4 bytes out of 100K bytes is not very important.

Another scenario would be a library supplied in binary form that requires this symbol and leaves it to the function to log or not log.

Anything other then _NONE means some means of outputting debug log messages exists - and the selection where you want the output to goto the APPLICATION uart - is the second case - an example is the CERT_LOG feature, where the log must go to the cli console for certification reasons.
